content:
First, observe that “adjacent” in a \(3\times3\) grid means sharing a side.  On the numbers \(1,2,\dots,9\) there are 5 odds and 4 evens.  Any side‐adjacency forcing different parities is exactly the usual checkerboard‐colouring constraint.  A \(3\times3\) checkerboard has 5 squares of one colour (say the “black’’ squares \((i+j)\) even) and 4 of the other.  Hence the only way to make every orthogonal adjacency go odd–even is to put the 5 odd numbers on the 5 black squares and the 4 even numbers on the 4 white squares.  

Number of raw labellings satisfying the parity‐alternation is
\[
5!\;\times\;4!\;=\;120\cdot24\;=\;2880.
\]
Now we identify two labellings if one is obtained from the other by a rotation (by \(0^\circ,90^\circ,180^\circ,\) or \(270^\circ\)).  By Burnside’s Lemma we need the average number of fixed labellings under these four rotations:

– The identity fixes all \(2880\).  
– A \(90^\circ\) or \(270^\circ\) rotation acts in 4‐cycles on the squares (with only the centre fixed), so no distinct labelling survives (one would need 4 equal numbers).  Thus 0 fixed.  
– A \(180^\circ\) rotation has the centre fixed and 4 disjoint transpositions; again no labelling of distinct numbers can be constant on each 2‐cycle, so 0 fixed.

Hence the number of orbits is
\[
\frac{2880+0+0+0}{4}\;=\;720.
\]
Answer: \(\boxed{720}\).
